slides.title: Stop Gluing Strings Together
slides.body: Concatenating text with `+` gets messy fast. **f-strings** let you drop values right inside a string — clean, readable, no fuss.
slides.tip: f-strings are the modern Python way — use them in all new code.

slides.title: Your First f-string
slides.body: Put `f` before the opening quote, then wrap any variable in `{curly braces}`. Python fills it in automatically.
slides.code:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
print(f"Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old.")
slides.output: Hello, Alice! You are 30 years old.
slides.tip: The f prefix is everything — without it, {name} prints literally.

slides.title: Expressions Go Inside Braces
slides.body: The `{}` in an f-string can hold **any expression** — math, method calls, even comparisons. Python evaluates it and inserts the result.

slides.title: Control How Numbers Look
slides.body: Add a colon after the variable name to set a **format spec**. `:.2f` gives exactly 2 decimal places; `:,` adds thousands separators.
slides.tip: Combine them: {:,.2f} gives both commas and 2 decimal places.

slides.title: You've Got String Formatting!
slides.body: f-strings turn messy string-building into clean, expressive one-liners. Add `:.2f` for decimals, `:,` for thousands, `:<10` / `:>10` to align columns — the braces do the rest.
slides.tip: If substitution isn't working, you almost certainly forgot the f prefix.
